title: sem capoeira eu nao posso viver
audio_url: data/music/sem capoeira eu nao posso viver.mp3
style_notes: Medium tempo, suitable for Defense and Attacks practice. Features traditional berimbau rhythms.
lyrics_pt:
Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
— Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
Eu sou como um passarinho
Que não pode voar sem asas
— Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
Capoeira é minha vida
É minha paixão, meu viver
— Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
Sem capoeira eu não posso viver
lyrics_en:
Without capoeira, I can’t live
Without capoeira, I can’t live
— Without capoeira, I can’t live
Without capoeira, I can’t live
I’m like a little bird
That can’t fly without wings
— Without capoeira, I can’t live
Without capoeira, I can’t live
Capoeira is my life
It’s my passion, my way of being
— Without capoeira, I can’t live
Without capoeira, I can’t live
historical_note: This song is a traditional capoeira anthem that expresses the deep connection practitioners feel to the art form, rooted in the Afro-Brazilian communities of Bahia. It reflects the post-abolition era when capoeira became a symbol of resistance and cultural identity for freed slaves and their descendants. Often sung in rodas to inspire dedication, it underscores how capoeira is not just a physical practice but a way of life, embodying freedom and resilience. Its call-and-response structure fosters unity among players.

title: Me leva na Bahia
style_notes: Slow berimbau-heavy track, ideal for Foundation warm-up and basic movements.
lyrics_pt:
Me leva na Bahia, na Bahia
Vou conhecer a Fazenda Estiva
Na terra de Jacobina
Meu mestre veio de lá
Olha, eu vou na Bahia

Na Bahia, quero ver o tempero
Alcaruru e também o vatapá
Olha, eu vou na Bahia

Casarão de pedra, que foi cativeiro de escravo
Onde um navio negreiro chegava do mar
Olha, eu vou na Bahia

Em macumba, no pé de roça velho
Na casa de pajelança, o axé, o pó-pongá
Olha, eu vou na Bahia

Me apaixona a cultura brasileira
É terra de mestre de umbanda
E também da capoeira
Olha, eu vou na Bahia
lyrics_en:
Take me to Bahia, to Bahia
I want to see Fazenda Estiva
In the land of Jacobina
My master came from there
Look, I’m going to Bahia

In Bahia, I want to taste the flavors
Alcaruru and also vatapá
Look, I’m going to Bahia

Stone mansion, once a slave prison
Where a slave ship arrived from the sea
Look, I’m going to Bahia

In macumba, at the foot of the old farm
In the house of pajelança, the axé, the pó-pongá
Look, I’m going to Bahia

I’m in love with Brazilian culture
It’s the land of umbanda masters
And also of capoeira
Look, I’m going to Bahia
historical_note: This song reflects the deep connection between capoeira and the cultural heritage of Bahia, the heartland of Afro-Brazilian traditions. It references specific elements like vatapá (a traditional dish) and Jacobina (a city in Bahia), grounding the song in the region’s history and geography, where capoeira emerged among enslaved Africans and their descendants during the colonial period. The mention of cativeiros (slave quarters) and navios negreiros (slave ships) evokes the painful legacy of slavery, while celebrating the resilience of Afro-Brazilian spiritual practices like macumba and umbanda. In the roda, this song often serves as a warm-up or reflective piece, reminding players of capoeira’s roots in resistance and cultural survival.

title: Carolina Soares - Vol 3 - 04 Capoeira não pode parar
audio_url: data/music/Carolina Soares - Vol 3 - 04 Capoeira não pode parar.mp3
style_notes: Energetic medium tempo, drum-heavy rhythm suitable for flow practice.
lyrics_pt:
Capoeira não pode parar
Capoeira não pode morrer
É do meu povo
E sem ela não posso viver

— Capoeira não pode parar
— Capoeira não pode morrer
— É do meu povo
— E sem ela não posso viver
lyrics_en:
Capoeira can’t stop
Capoeira can’t die
It’s of my people
And without it, I can’t survive

— Capoeira can’t stop
— Capoeira can’t die
— It’s of my people
— And without it, I can’t survive
historical_note: This song reflects the deep cultural connection between capoeira and the Afro-Brazilian community, particularly in Bahia, where capoeira emerged as a form of resistance and expression during the slavery era. It emphasizes the resilience and enduring spirit of capoeira as a vital part of identity and survival for descendants of the African diaspora. Often sung in rodas to inspire persistence and unity, it serves as a reminder of the art’s role in overcoming oppression. While not tied to a specific master or date, its themes resonate with post-abolition (late 19th century) struggles for cultural preservation.

title: Eu Já Vivo Enjoado ⧸ Corridos de Angola
audio_url: data/music/Eu Já Vivo Enjoado ⧸ Corridos de Angola.mp3
style_notes: Extended Angola corridos track with medium tempo, great for longer practice sessions.
lyrics_pt:
Eu já vivo enjoado
De viver aqui na terra
Meu desejo é ir-me embora
Pra bem longe desta guerra
— Eu já vivo enjoado
— De viver aqui na terra

Ô senhor amigo meu
Diga o dia do meu cantar
Quem é dono não se incomoda
Quem não é, se incomoda
— Ô senhor amigo meu
— Diga o dia do meu cantar

É hora, é hora
Quem não se embora
Quem se embora, meu Deus
Que viva meu mestre
— É hora, é hora
— Quem não se embora

Que dê a volta ao mundo
E que o mundo dê a mim
Mulher, como vai, como tá?
Não sei quem vai vencer
— Que dê a volta ao mundo
— E que o mundo dê a mim

Ô canarim da Alemanha
Bora, da Lua, quem sabe é o clarão
Ô canarim da Alemanha
Curió, curió, curió
— Ô canarim da Alemanha
— Bora, da Lua, quem sabe é o clarão

Se eu quero recair
Ela chega na venda
Ela manda botar
É do camboatá
— Se eu quero recair
— Ela chega na venda

Vida, salto mortal tá
É a razão que me quer
Aprender a ideia
No chante que for embora
— Vida, salto mortal tá
— É a razão que me quer

Eu não fico
Apanha a laranja
No chão que meu amor
Meu amor for embora
— Eu não fico
— Apanha a laranja
lyrics_en:
I’m already fed up
With living here on earth
My wish is to leave
Far away from this war
— I’m already fed up
— With living here on earth

Oh, my dear friend
Tell me the day of my song
Those who own don’t mind
Those who don’t, they care
— Oh, my dear friend
— Tell me the day of my song

It’s time, it’s time
Whoever doesn’t leave
Whoever leaves, my God
Long live my master
— It’s time, it’s time
— Whoever doesn’t leave

May they travel the world
And may the world come to me
Woman, how are you, how’s it going?
I don’t know who will win
— May they travel the world
— And may the world come to me

Oh, canary from Germany
Come on, from the Moon, who knows, it’s the glow
Oh, canary from Germany
Curió, curió, curió
— Oh, canary from Germany
— Come on, from the Moon, who knows, it’s the glow

If I want to fall back
She arrives at the shop
She orders to put it down
It’s from camboatá
— If I want to fall back
— She arrives at the shop

Life, a deadly leap it is
It’s the reason that wants me
To learn the idea
In the chant that leaves
— Life, a deadly leap it is
— It’s the reason that wants me

I won’t stay
Pick up the orange
On the ground, for my love
If my love leaves
— I won’t stay
— Pick up the orange
historical_note: Eu Já Vivo Enjoado is a traditional corrido often sung in Angola-style capoeira rodas, known for its slower, more strategic pace. Originating from the African diaspora in Bahia, Brazil, these corridos carry layers of meaning, often expressing longing, resistance, and the struggles of life under oppression, likely dating to the post-abolition era. The song’s references to leaving and war may metaphorically address the desire to escape hardship or return to ancestral roots. In the roda, it sets a reflective tone, encouraging players to focus on cunning and patience over aggression.

title: A Hora É Essa ⧸ Um Dia na Senzala
audio_url: data/music/A Hora É Essa ⧸ Um Dia na Senzala.mp3
style_notes: Medium-fast tempo with drum emphasis, suitable for attack sequences.
lyrics_pt:
A hora é essa, camará
A hora é essa, camará
— A hora é essa, camará
— A hora é essa, camará
Um dia na senzala, camará
Um dia na senzala, camará
— Um dia na senzala, camará
— Um dia na senzala, camará
Capoeira é luta, camará
Capoeira é luta, camará
— Capoeira é luta, camará
— Capoeira é luta, camará
lyrics_en:
The time is now, my friend
The time is now, my friend
— The time is now, my friend
— The time is now, my friend
One day in the slave quarters, my friend
One day in the slave quarters, my friend
— One day in the slave quarters, my friend
— One day in the slave quarters, my friend
Capoeira is a fight, my friend
Capoeira is a fight, my friend
— Capoeira is a fight, my friend
— Capoeira is a fight, my friend
historical_note: A Hora É Essa / Um Dia na Senzala is a traditional capoeira song that reflects the urgency and resistance of enslaved Africans in Brazil, particularly in the context of Bahia's senzala (slave quarters) during the colonial period. It evokes the historical struggle and the transformation of capoeira from a hidden form of combat into a cultural expression of freedom. The song is often sung in rodas to inspire energy and focus during intense games, emphasizing the readiness to act and the roots of capoeira in oppression and liberation. Its call-and-response structure fosters unity and rhythm among players.

title: Paranaue-Capoeira
style_notes: Fast energetic track, ideal for Aerials and Floreios practice.
lyrics_pt:
Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
— Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
Vou me embora, vou me embora, Paraná
— Vou me embora, vou me embora, Paraná
Não vou ficar, não vou ficar, Paraná
— Não vou ficar, não vou ficar, Paraná
Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
— Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
lyrics_en:
Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
— Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
I’m leaving, I’m leaving, Paraná
— I’m leaving, I’m leaving, Paraná
I won’t stay, I won’t stay, Paraná
— I won’t stay, I won’t stay, Paraná
Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
— Paranauê, Paranauê, Paraná
historical_note: Paranauê is one of the most iconic songs in capoeira, deeply rooted in the Afro-Brazilian traditions of Bahia. Its origins are often traced to the 19th century, reflecting the struggles and resilience of enslaved Africans and their descendants during and after the abolition era in Brazil (1888). The term 'Paraná' may refer to the Paraná River or a symbolic journey, possibly alluding to escape or migration. In the roda, it energizes players, often accompanying fast-paced, acrobatic games with its driving rhythm.

title: Berimbau Viola
style_notes: Slow berimbau viola track, perfect for Foundation warm-up and ginga practice.
lyrics_pt:
Berimbau viola, meu amigo
Berimbau viola, meu irmão
Toca o berimbau, que eu quero jogar
Toca o berimbau, que eu quero dançar
— Berimbau viola, meu amigo
— Berimbau viola, meu irmão
Toca o berimbau, que eu quero jogar
Toca o berimbau, que eu quero dançar
Berimbau viola, me leva pro mar
Berimbau viola, me leva pra lá
— Berimbau viola, meu amigo
— Berimbau viola, meu irmão
lyrics_en:
Berimbau viola, my friend
Berimbau viola, my brother
Play the berimbau, I want to play
Play the berimbau, I want to dance
— Berimbau viola, my friend
— Berimbau viola, my brother
Play the berimbau, I want to play
Play the berimbau, I want to dance
Berimbau viola, take me to the sea
Berimbau viola, take me over there
— Berimbau viola, my friend
— Berimbau viola, my brother
historical_note: Berimbau Viola is a traditional capoeira song that pays homage to the berimbau, the iconic single-stringed instrument central to capoeira rodas. Originating in Bahia, Brazil, with roots in the African diaspora, the song reflects the deep connection between music and movement in capoeira, likely emerging during the post-abolition era when capoeira transitioned from a hidden practice to a cultural expression. It is often sung during warm-ups or slower-paced rodas to set a reflective tone, emphasizing camaraderie and the spiritual guidance of the berimbau in the game. The call-and-response structure fosters unity among players, echoing the communal spirit of Afro-Brazilian traditions.

title: Acarajé - João Grilo AC
style_notes: Medium tempo track with traditional capoeira rhythms, suitable for Defense practice.
lyrics_pt:
Solta todo aquele axé que tá dentro de você
Deixa ele te guiar, te levar e te envolver
Solta o seu axé que tá dentro do seu corpo
Segura a saudade, moça, vem que o berimbau vai tocar

Acarajé, é igual na roda
Tem que se defender pra fazer o axé
Acarajé, a palma do berimbau tem que importar
Pra fazer a roda se emocionar

As palmas juntas fazem o axé nascer
Fazem a roda crescer pro capoeira vadiar
Acarajé, te defender no braço, disso não vai faltar
Tem sabedoria ancestral, sua experiência a compartilhar
lyrics_en:
Release all that axé that’s inside of you
Let it guide you, take you, and wrap you up
Release the axé that’s within your body
Hold that longing, girl, come, the berimbau is about to play

Acarajé, it’s just like in the roda
You’ve got to defend yourself to bring out the axé
Acarajé, the rhythm of the berimbau has to matter
To make the roda feel the emotion

Clapping together makes the axé come alive
Makes the roda grow for the capoeira to roam
Acarajé, defending with your arm, that won’t be lacking
There’s ancestral wisdom, your experience to share
historical_note: This song, 'Acarajé,' draws on the cultural symbolism of acarajé, a traditional dish from Bahia, Brazil, often associated with Afro-Brazilian religious practices and the strength of the African diaspora. It reflects the energy (axé) of capoeira, emphasizing defense and community in the roda, a key aspect of practice in Bahian traditions. Likely a contemporary composition inspired by traditional themes, it connects the physicality of capoeira with spiritual and cultural roots, encouraging players to bring their full energy and ancestral wisdom into the game.

title: Capoeira Camará
style_notes: Fast energetic track, designed for Aerials and Floreios sequences.
lyrics_pt:
Capoeira, camará
Capoeira, camará
— Capoeira, camará
— Capoeira, camará
Jogo bonito de se ver
Dentro da roda a valer
— Capoeira, camará
— Capoeira, camará
É luta, é dança, é tradição
Herança de nossa nação
— Capoeira, camará
— Capoeira, camará
lyrics_en:
Capoeira, my friend
Capoeira, my friend
— Capoeira, my friend
— Capoeira, my friend
A beautiful game to watch
Inside the roda, full of worth
— Capoeira, my friend
— Capoeira, my friend
It’s a fight, it’s a dance, it’s tradition
A legacy of our nation
— Capoeira, my friend
— Capoeira, my friend
historical_note: Capoeira Camará is a traditional capoeira song often sung to energize the roda during dynamic and acrobatic sequences. The term 'camará' is a friendly address, reflecting the camaraderie and community spirit central to capoeira, rooted in the African diaspora of Bahia, Brazil. Likely emerging in the post-abolition era of the late 19th century, it celebrates the art as both a martial form and cultural expression, encouraging players to showcase skill and flair. In the roda, it often signals a call for high-energy movements and connection between players.

title: Berimbau Viola Soul
style_notes: Slow berimbau viola with soulful rhythm, ideal for Foundation and warm-up.
lyrics_pt:
Berimbau viola, meu amigo
Toca um som que vem de longe
— Berimbau viola, meu amigo
— Toca um som que vem de longe
Berimbau, tu és minha alma
Carrega a história do meu povo
— Berimbau, tu és minha alma
— Carrega a história do meu povo
Toca devagar, toca com dor
Sente a luta no meu suor
— Toca devagar, toca com dor
— Sente a luta no meu suor
lyrics_en:
Berimbau viola, my friend
Play a sound that comes from afar
— Berimbau viola, my friend
— Play a sound that comes from afar
Berimbau, you are my soul
You carry the history of my people
— Berimbau, you are my soul
— You carry the history of my people
Play slowly, play with pain
Feel the struggle in my sweat
— Play slowly, play with pain
— Feel the struggle in my sweat
historical_note: Berimbau Viola Soul, as a variation of traditional berimbau-led songs, reflects the deep connection between capoeira and the berimbau, the iconic single-string instrument of African origin. In the roda, such songs often set a contemplative tone during warm-ups or foundational training, emphasizing the spiritual and historical weight of capoeira as a form of resistance in Bahia, Brazil. They evoke the African diaspora’s enduring memory of struggle and resilience, particularly from the slavery era through to post-abolition times. This style of song guides players to connect emotionally with the rhythm before engaging in physical play.

title: Pomba Voou
style_notes: Medium-fast tempo track, suitable for Flow and advanced Defense practice.
lyrics_pt:
Ai, ai, ai, chê!
Joga bonito que eu quero ver
Ai, ai, ai, chê!
Joga bonito que eu quero aprender
Ai, ai, ai, chê!
Joga bonito que eu quero ver
Ai, ai, ai, chê!
Esse joga bonito que eu quero aprender
— Joga bonito que eu quero ver
— Joga bonito que eu quero aprender
lyrics_en:
Hey, hey, hey, chê!
Play beautifully, I want to see
Hey, hey, hey, chê!
Play beautifully, I want to learn
Hey, hey, hey, chê!
Play beautifully, I want to see
Hey, hey, hey, chê!
This one plays beautifully, I want to learn
— Play beautifully, I want to see
— Play beautifully, I want to learn
historical_note: Pomba Voou is a traditional capoeira song often sung in rodas to encourage fluid, beautiful movements and to inspire learning through observation. Its origins are rooted in the Afro-Brazilian communities of Bahia, reflecting the oral tradition of the African diaspora where songs served as both entertainment and education. The call-and-response structure fosters a sense of community and dialogue in the roda, urging players to showcase their best skills while inviting others to grow. It is particularly associated with the Angola style, emphasizing grace and storytelling through movement.
